title: 👑 Elite Challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: The Verb To Be

questions.prompt: Read: "Lina and Omar are in the same class. Lina is from Sfax. Omar is from Tunis." Which sentence is TRUE?
questions.explanation: "In the same class" means they are classmates (are, because Lina and Omar = they). (b) swaps the cities — Lina is from Sfax. (c) contradicts the text — being in a class, Omar is a student. (d) contradicts "the same class".

options.text: There is a museum near the school.
There are two museums near the school.
Is there a museum near the school?
There are a museum near the school.

questions.explanation: The error is (d): with a singular noun (a museum) you must use there is, not there are. The word a signals one thing. (a), (b) and (c) all match the verb to the number correctly.

questions.prompt: "Where are your keys?" Choose the most natural reply.
questions.explanation: Keys is plural = they, so the reply is They're (they are) on the table. "Their" is possessive and "There" shows place — neither can mean they are. "It's" is singular and cannot stand for plural keys.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Everyone in the two teams ___ ready to play."
questions.explanation: Everyone is grammatically singular and takes is, even though it refers to many people and "teams" is plural: Everyone ... is ready. The nearby plural teams is a deliberate trap.
With I, the only negative form is I'm not (I'm not your teacher): there is no "amn't", and aren't is for you/we/they. "I not am" has the wrong word order — not goes after the verb.

questions.prompt: Which question-and-answer pair is completely correct?
questions.explanation: Only (b) is correct: Is he…? — No, he isn't. (a) wrongly contracts a positive short answer (it must be "Yes, I am"). (c) uses singular is with they (should be "they are"). (d) has no verb (should be "No, you aren't / you're not").
